docs.title: NixOS Homeprod
docs.text: Managing a "Homeprod" configuration using NixOS.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
For the longest time I ran a Kubernetes cluster at home for almost all of my 'production' applications and realised how much actual overhead was being introduced with the supporting tooling to manage the cluster. For example I also ran:

  • Longhorn for persistent storage
  • Prometheus stack for monitoring
  • MetalLB, Traefik, external DNS, cert-manager for Ingress
  • Kured, system-upgrade

This is just to get the cluster in a daily manageable state.

So it is time to simplify, and with that I like the defined state of Kubernetes, so why not keep to that with using NixOS to manage containers and services?

docs.title: Repository
docs.text: The configuration lives at nikdoof/nixos-homeprod on GitHub.

docs.title: afp-01: Apple File Sharing
docs.text: afp-01 is a NixOS microVM (CID 11) on hyp-01, running on VLAN 101. It provides AFP file sharing via Netatalk and AppleTalk printing via papd for legacy Mac clients.

docs.title: Network
docs.text: Property Value Platform microVM, CID 11, VLAN 101 IPv4 10.101.3.30/16 IPv6 2001:8b0:bd9:101::3:30/64 ULA fddd:d00f:dab0:101::3:30/64

docs.title: Netatalk (AFP file sharing)
docs.text:
Netatalk 4.4.1 serves AFP volumes to legacy Mac clients over AppleTalk and TCP:

Volume Path Access Software Archive /persist/netatalk/shares/archive Read-only (nobody), write (nikdoof) Dropbox /persist/netatalk/shares/dropbox Read-write (anyone) Transfer /persist/netatalk/shares/transfer nikdoof only Data /persist/netatalk/shares/data Read-only (nobody), write (nikdoof)

docs.title: AppleTalk daemon (atalkd)
docs.text: The atalkd service provides AppleTalk Phase 2 routing, required by Netatalk for legacy protocol support (DDP, PAP, etc.).

docs.title: PAP printing (papd)
docs.text: The papd service enables AppleTalk printing to an HP LaserJet 200 M251n. CUPS is also enabled for IPP printing.

docs.title: GlobalTalk scraper
docs.text: The GlobalTalk scraper (services.globaltalk.scrape) fetches AppleTalk network trends and writes results to /persist/netatalk/shares/data/globaltalk.json, making them available on the Data volume. Metrics are enabled for Prometheus collection.

docs.title: Dropbox notify
docs.text: A notification service watches the Dropbox volume and posts to the Mastodon instance (social.doofnet.uk) when new files are added. Currently runs in dry-run mode.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text: Netatalk shares and CNID databases are stored under /persist/netatalk/.

docs.title: Service summary
docs.text: Service Package Purpose netatalk netatalk 4.4.1 AFP file server atalkd netatalk AppleTalk Phase 2 routing papd netatalk AppleTalk printing avahi avahi mDNS/Bonjour service discovery globaltalk-scraper globaltalk-scraper flake AppleTalk trend scraper cupd cups IPP printing dropbox-notify (custom) Watch dropbox and notify via Mastodon alloy grafana-alloy Metrics and log shipping

docs.title: Base Host Standards
docs.text: Common standards that apply to all physical NixOS hosts in the homelab.

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
Hardware is being repurposed from old Kubernetes worker nodes.

Device RAM OS disk OS HP Prodesk 600 G3 DM 16 GB 128 GB SATA NixOS 25.11

docs.title: Local storage
docs.text:
All local storage is provided under /srv:

  • /srv/data — local SSD/NVMe, mapped to the data disk
  • /srv/data/<application> — per-application directory
  • /srv/data/<application>/<mount> — per-mount-point subdirectory (e.g. /srv/data/sonarr/config)
  • /srv/cluster — clustered storage (CephFS, GlusterFS, etc.), not mounted directly

docs.title: Remote storage
docs.text:
Mounts for remote storage are placed under /mnt:

  • /mnt/<hostname>/<share> — network or cluster-native mounts (e.g. /mnt/nas-03/media)

docs.title: Backups
docs.text: Backups are managed by borgmatic and stored on a remote Hetzner Storage Box. Only directories under /srv/data are backed up.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
All hosts run Grafana Alloy as a doofnet.server node, which:

  • Collects host metrics via prometheus.exporter.unix and forwards them to Prometheus
  • Ships systemd journal logs to Loki
  • Writes a nixos_flake_revision textfile metric on every activation so the deployed flake revision is visible in Grafana

docs.title: DNS Servers
docs.text:
The homelab runs four BIND9 DNS servers configured via a shared doofnet.bind NixOS module: two internal servers (ns-01, ns-02) that serve all zones and act as recursive resolvers, and two public AWS secondaries (ns-03, ns-04) that serve only externally-delegated zones.

Host Role Platform Address (IPv4) Address (IPv6) ns-01 Primary Raspberry Pi 3, aarch64 10.101.1.2 2001:8b0:bd9:101::2 ns-02 Secondary microVM on hyp-01 (VLAN 101) 10.101.1.3 2001:8b0:bd9:101::3 ns-03 Public secondary AWS EC2, eu-west-1 (x86_64) 52.19.64.4 — ns-04 Public secondary AWS EC2, eu-west-2 (x86_64) 16.60.149.205 —

ns-01 and ns-02 also carry a ULA address on the private VLAN:

  • ns-01: fddd:d00f:dab0:101::2
  • ns-02: fddd:d00f:dab0:101::3

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
All four servers run the same BIND configuration, toggled between primary and secondary mode by a single option. The primary holds the authoritative copy of every zone; secondaries receive zone transfers from the primary.

ns-01 is deployed as a Raspberry Pi 3 SD card image. Because it is aarch64, the nix-community.cachix.org binary cache is configured to pull pre-built binaries during cross-compiled deployments from an x86_64 builder.

ns-02 is a NixOS microVM (CID 13) running on hyp-01. It serves all zones and acts as a hot standby internal resolver. See docs/hyp-01.md for details on the microVM platform.

ns-03 and ns-04 are AWS EC2 instances (eu-west-1 and eu-west-2 respectively) running in secondary mode with publicOnly = true. They only serve zones whose NS records include ns-03.doofnet.uk. or ns-04.doofnet.uk., providing public authoritative DNS with geographic redundancy. They do not act as recursive resolvers. Because they cannot reach the internal primary directly, zone transfers use the gateway's public NAT IP (81.187.48.147 → 10.101.1.2).

docs.title: Module: doofnet.bind (modules/doofnet/bind/)
docs.text:
Enabling the module on a host:

doofnet.bind = {
  enable = true;
  mode = "primary";   # or "secondary"
};

For public-facing secondaries that cannot reach the primary on its internal address:

doofnet.bind = {
  enable = true;
  mode = "secondary";
  publicOnly = true;   # serve only externally-delegated zones
  masters = [ "81.187.48.147" ];  # gateway's public NAT IP → 10.101.1.2
};

publicOnly filters the active zone list to only those zones whose NS records contain ns-03.doofnet.uk. or ns-04.doofnet.uk.. masters overrides the default internal primary addresses for hosts that reach ns-01 via NAT.

docs.title: Zone rendering
docs.text:
Zones are written as Nix expressions using the dns flake library. Every .nix file in modules/doofnet/bind/zones/ (except default.nix and lib.nix) is automatically loaded as a zone, with the filename (minus .nix) used as the zone name. Each zone file receives dns (the upstream library) and zlib (local helpers exporting mkSOA, internalNS, publicNS).

Each zone declares:

  • zoneData — DNS records, in dns.lib's data model.
  • dynamic (optional) — DDNS configuration, structured as Nix data:
  • dynamic.enable = false (default) — static zone, no DDNS.
  • dynamic.enable = true; dynamic.protect = []; — blanket allow-update for the DDNS TSIG key.
  • dynamic.enable = true; dynamic.protect = [ "name1" "name2" … ]; — update-policy that denies updates to the listed names (relative to the zone) and grants everything else as zonesub. Used to shield statically-defined records from DHCP overwrites.

The module derives whether a zone is publicly delegated by checking its NS list against the known public secondary hostnames (ns-03.doofnet.uk., ns-04.doofnet.uk.); public zones are transferred to ns-03/ns-04, all others stay on ns-02.

Static zones are written to the Nix store at build time and BIND reads them directly. Dynamic zones (dynamic.enable = true) need a writable file so BIND can journal updates; the file is copied to /var/lib/bind/zones/<name>.zone. A bind-update-zones systemd service ordered before bind.service detects SOA serial changes between Nix rebuilds and merges the new static records with the dynamic DDNS records preserved from the on-disk zone file (backing up the old one and discarding the now-stale .jnl journal).

docs.title: Zone transfer
docs.text:
Primary sends transfers to:

  • 10.101.1.3 / 2001:8b0:bd9:101::3 (ns-02) — all zones
  • 52.19.64.4 (ns-03) and 16.60.149.205 (ns-04) — public zones only (those with ns-03/04 in NS records)

Zone transfers are denied by default (allow-transfer { none; }); each zone's slaves list (derived by the module) provides the per-zone exception.

docs.title: DDNS updates
docs.text:
Dynamic DNS updates arrive from kea-dhcp-ddns on the gateway using TSIG key doofnet-dhcp-updates (HMAC-SHA256). The key is stored in an age-encrypted secret and included at runtime via BIND's include directive — it is never written to the Nix store.

An acl "doofnet-dhcp-updates" block matches requests that present a valid TSIG signature. The module turns the zone's dynamic attribute into BIND's allow-update (open) or update-policy (with a protect list) directive — see Zone rendering above.

docs.title: DNS-over-TLS
docs.text:
BIND listens on port 853 (TCP) with TLS on all interfaces. The certificate is obtained via ACME DNS-01 challenge using the DigitalOcean API (the domain's public NS is managed there). ACME renewal triggers a BIND service reload. The TLS configuration is defined in BIND's tls local-tls block referencing the certificate at:

/var/lib/acme/<hostname>.int.doofnet.uk/{fullchain,key}.pem

docs.title: Recursion and caching
docs.text:
ns-01 and ns-02 act as recursive resolvers for internal clients (ns-03/04 do not). Recursion is permitted for:

  • 127.0.0.0/8 and ::1 (loopback)
  • All internal networks (defined in modules/doofnet/const.nix as allNetworks)

External clients receive REFUSED. Notable resolver settings:

Setting Value Effect dnssec-validation auto Validates DNSSEC; uses built-in trust anchors qname-minimisation strict Sends only the minimum labels needed per delegation step minimal-responses yes Omits unnecessary additional section records max-cache-size 256 MiB Caps memory used by the answer cache max-cache-ttl 86400 s Caps positive cache entries at 24 h max-ncache-ttl 3600 s Caps negative cache entries at 1 h prefetch 2 9 Prefetches cache entries with ≤2 s left if queried ≥9× stale-answer-enable yes Serves stale answers during upstream outages stale-answer-ttl 30 s Stale entries served for up to 30 s while refreshing

Forwarders are explicitly empty — servers resolve iteratively from the root.

docs.title: Rate limiting
docs.text: Responses are rate-limited to 10 per second per client, with a 5-second window. All internal networks are exempt, so rate limiting only applies to external clients (and is primarily a defence against amplification attacks).

docs.title: Response Policy Zone (RPZ)
docs.text:
A local rpz zone provides DNS-level overrides enforced with break-dnssec yes. Current overrides:

Name Resolves to Purpose svc-prod-ingress-external.doofnet.uk 10.101.3.20 Redirect internal clients to local ingress tester.mfg.cobaltmicro.com 10.101.3.104 Local override for third-party hostname

The RPZ zone is served by both primary and secondary. To block a domain, add it with CNAME = [ "." ]; to pass it through the policy, use CNAME = [ "rpz-passthru." ].

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
BIND logs to two files under /var/log/named/:

File Categories Retention security.log default, security, dnssec, query-errors, xfer-in/out, notify 3 files × 20 MiB queries.log queries 5 files × 100 MiB

Noisy low-signal categories (lame-servers, edns-disabled, rpz) are sent to a null channel.

Both log files are tailed by Grafana Alloy and shipped to Loki. The Alloy pipeline parses each format:

  • Query log: extracts client_ip, client_port, qname, qtype, qclass, flags and stores them as structured metadata. qtype and qclass become Loki stream labels.
  • Security log: extracts category and severity as stream labels.

Alloy's supplementary group membership (named) grants read access to the log directory without widening file permissions.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: A prometheus-bind-exporter instance runs on 127.0.0.1:9119, scraping BIND's statistics channel at 127.0.0.1:8053. The exporter is not exposed externally; Alloy scrapes it locally and ships metrics to Prometheus.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
Use scripts/update-ns.sh to deploy DNS servers in the correct order (primary first, then public secondaries). The script SSHes to svc-02 and runs nixos-rebuild switch there against the published GitHub flake, so changes must be pushed before running.

# Deploy all servers (ns-01, ns-03, ns-04) in order
./scripts/update-ns.sh

# Deploy specific servers only
./scripts/update-ns.sh ns-01
./scripts/update-ns.sh ns-03 ns-04

Environment variables to override defaults:

Variable Default Purpose BUILD_HOST svc-02.int.doofnet.uk Host that runs nixos-rebuild FLAKE github:nikdoof/nixos-homeprod Flake reference to deploy from

The script resolves each host to its FQDN for --target-host (ns-01/02 use .int.doofnet.uk; ns-03/04 use .doofnet.uk) while using the short name as the flake config key.

docs.title: Forward zones
docs.text:
Zone NS Dynamic DDNS Notes int.doofnet.uk ns-01, ns-02 yes policy Protected static records + open DDNS for clients pub.doofnet.uk ns-01, ns-02 yes yes Public VLAN clients lab.doofnet.uk ns-01, ns-02 yes yes Lab VLAN clients ha.doofnet.uk ns-01, ns-02 yes yes Home automation VLAN clients svc.doofnet.uk ns-01, ns-02 no — Service endpoints; wildcard *.svc.doofnet.uk → 10.101.3.20 service.arpa ns-01, ns-02 no — Special-use (RFC 6761) local resolution rpz ns-01, ns-02 no — Response Policy Zone

svc.doofnet.uk has a 300-second TTL (shorter than the default 3600 s) and includes specific overrides for grafana, unifi, loki, and prometheus pointing to 10.101.3.21.

docs.text: Zone Covers Also on ns-03/04 DDNS 1.0.1.0.9.d.b…ip6.arpa VLAN 101 2001:8b0:bd9:101: yes yes 2.0.1.0.9.d.b…ip6.arpa VLAN 102 2001:8b0:bd9:102: yes yes 4.0.1.0.9.d.b…ip6.arpa VLAN 104 2001:8b0:bd9:104: yes yes 5.0.1.0.9.d.b…ip6.arpa VLAN 105 2001:8b0:bd9:105: yes yes 6.0.1.0.9.d.b…ip6.arpa VLAN 106 2001:8b0:bd9:106: yes yes 0.b.a.d.f.0.0.d.d.d.d.f.ip6.arpa ULA fddd:d00f:dab0::/48 no no

docs.title: Adding a new zone
docs.text:
1. Create modules/doofnet/bind/zones/<zone-name>.nix. Filename (minus .nix) is the zone name. Take { dns, zlib, ... } and use zlib.mkSOA <serial>, zlib.internalNS (or zlib.publicNS for publicly delegated zones).
  2. Set the SOA serial to today's date in YYYYMMDDnn format.
  3. For DDNS, add dynamic.enable = true; (blanket allow) or dynamic = { enable = true; protect = [ ... ]; }; (update-policy with denied names).
  4. Rebuild and deploy ns-01; ns-02 (and ns-03/ns-04 if publicNS) pick up the new zone via AXFR automatically.

docs.title: Adding a static record to int.doofnet.uk
docs.text:
1. Add the subdomain to int.doofnet.uk.nix under subdomains.
  2. Add the relative name to dynamic.protect to shield it from DDNS overwrites.
  3. Increment the SOA serial.
  4. Rebuild ns-01; on the next bind restart bind-update-zones detects the serial change and merges the new static records with preserved DDNS state.

docs.title: Service summary
docs.text: Service Package Purpose named (BIND9) bind Authoritative DNS + recursive resolver bind-update-zones (custom systemd oneshot) Merges Nix zone changes with live DDNS journals prometheus-bind-exporter prometheus-bind-exporter BIND statistics → Prometheus alloy grafana-alloy Metrics and log shipping

docs.title: grf-01: Grafana Dashboard
docs.text: grf-01 is a NixOS microVM (CID 15) on hyp-01, running on VLAN 101. It hosts a dedicated Grafana instance primarily used for visualising GlobalTalk scraper data, with Prometheus as its datasource.

docs.text: Property Value Platform microVM, CID 15, VLAN 101 IPv4 10.101.3.31/16 IPv6 2001:8b0:bd9:101::3:31/64 ULA fddd:d00f:dab0:101::3:31/64 DNS suffix globaltalk.doofnet.uk

docs.text: Service Package Purpose grafana grafana Dashboarding UI alloy grafana-alloy Metrics shipping (self-scrape)

docs.title: gw: Gateway / Router
docs.text: gw is the network gateway for the homelab, running NixOS on an HP Prodesk 400 G4 SFF. It provides routing, firewalling, DHCP, DNS, NTP, and VPN services for all VLANs.

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
HP Prodesk 400 G4 SFF (Intel Kaby Lake). Three network interfaces are relevant:

Interface Role enp2s0 Management (out-of-band access to gw) enp3s0f0 Internal trunk (carries all VLANs) enp3s0f1 WAN (uplink to CityFibre ONT)

docs.title: VLANs
docs.text:
All internal traffic is carried as 802.1Q tagged frames on enp3s0f0. Five VLANs are defined:

VLAN Interface IPv4 prefix IPv6 prefix DNS zone Purpose 101 vlan-private 10.101.0.0/16 2001:8b0:bd9:101::/64 int.doofnet.uk Internal infrastructure 102 vlan-public 10.102.0.0/16 2001:8b0:bd9:102::/64 pub.doofnet.uk Public-facing services (NATed) 104 vlan-lab 10.104.0.0/16 2001:8b0:bd9:104::/64 lab.doofnet.uk Lab / experimental 105 vlan-ha — 2001:8b0:bd9:105::/64 ha.doofnet.uk Home automation / IoT (IPv6 only) 106 vlan-hosted 217.169.25.8/29 2001:8b0:bd9:106::/64 — Internet-facing, publicly routed

The hosted VLAN (106) is not NATed — traffic to/from 217.169.25.8/29 is routed directly by the ISP. All other VLANs use IPv4 masquerade NAT via the PPPoE interface.

Private (101) and Lab (104) also receive a ULA prefix for stable internal IPv6 addressing independent of the ISP:

VLAN ULA prefix 101 fddd:d00f:dab0:101::/64 104 fddd:d00f:dab0:104::/64

docs.title: Management interface
docs.text: enp2s0 carries a static address (10.101.3.23/16) as a fallback management path if the VLAN trunk goes down. Routes for the private subnet are installed with a high metric (2048) so the trunk VLAN is preferred under normal operation.

docs.title: WAN — PPPoE
docs.text:
The WAN connection is PPPoE over CityFibre fibre. enp3s0f1 carries a single VLAN 911 (vlan-wan), and pppd runs the aaisp peer on top of it, producing a ppp0 interface.

IPv6 on WAN is obtained via DHCPv6 prefix delegation and RA from the ISP; the router accepts the delegated prefix and distributes /64s to internal VLANs via Kea DHCPv6. DNS from the ISP is not used (UseDNS = no).

PPPoE credentials are stored in an age-encrypted secret deployed to /etc/ppp/pap-secrets.

docs.title: DHCP — Kea DHCPv4 (services/dhcp4.nix)
docs.text:
Kea serves DHCPv4 on VLANs 101, 102, 104, and 105. Lease lifetime is 24 hours. All subnets send DNS update requests to the local kea-dhcp-ddns server (port 53001).

VLAN Pool Router DNS servers DNS suffix 101 10.101.2.1 – 10.101.2.254 10.101.1.1 10.101.1.2, 10.101.1.3 int.doofnet.uk 102 10.102.2.1 – 10.102.2.254 10.102.1.1 10.101.1.2, 10.101.1.3 pub.doofnet.uk 104 10.104.2.1 – 10.104.2.254 10.104.1.1 10.101.1.2, 10.101.1.3 lab.doofnet.uk 105 10.105.2.1 – 10.105.2.254 10.105.1.1 10.101.1.2, 10.101.1.3 ha.doofnet.uk

Static reservations on VLAN 101:

Hostname IP MAC svc-01 10.101.3.20 10:62:e5:14:61:84 svc-02 10.101.3.21 f4:39:09:3a:4d:a4 hyp-01 10.101.3.22 10:e7:c6:03:97:18

PXE boot is supported on VLANs 101 and 104. Client architecture option 93 selects the boot file:

  • 0x0000 (BIOS) → undionly.kpxe
  • 0x0007 / 0x0009 (UEFI) → ipxe.efi

The next-server for VLAN 101 is 10.101.3.21 (svc-02/JRouter); for VLAN 104 it is 10.101.3.102.

docs.title: DHCP — Kea DHCPv6 (services/dhcp6.nix)
docs.text:
Kea serves DHCPv6 on VLANs 101, 102, 104, and 105 (VLAN 106 uses SLAAC only). Leases are coordinated with DDNS.

VLAN Pool DNS servers 101 2001:8b0:bd9:101::2000 – ::2fff 2001:8b0:bd9:101::2 / ::3 102 2001:8b0:bd9:102::2000 – ::2fff 2001:8b0:bd9:101::2 / ::3 104 2001:8b0:bd9:104::2000 – ::2fff 2001:8b0:bd9:101::2 / ::3 105 2001:8b0:bd9:105::2000 – ::2fff 2001:8b0:bd9:101::2 / ::3

VLAN 101 also offers prefix delegation from 2001:8b0:bd9:200::/56, handing out /64s for clients that need their own subnet.

docs.title: Dynamic DNS — Kea DHCP-DDNS (services/ddns.nix)
docs.text:
kea-dhcp-ddns listens on 127.0.0.1:53001 and receives name-change requests from both Kea servers. It authenticates to the primary DNS server (10.101.1.2) using TSIG (HMAC-SHA256, key name doofnet-dhcp-updates).

Forward zones updated: int.doofnet.uk, pub.doofnet.uk, lab.doofnet.uk, ha.doofnet.uk

Reverse zones updated: 101.10.in-addr.arpa, 102.10.in-addr.arpa, 104.10.in-addr.arpa, 105.10.in-addr.arpa, and the corresponding IPv6 reverse zones for VLANs 101, 102, 104, and 106.

The TSIG secret is age-encrypted and injected at service start by an ExecStartPre script that rewrites the config into /run/kea/dhcp-ddns.conf, keeping the secret out of the Nix store.

docs.title: DNS — Unbound (services/dns.nix)
docs.text:
Unbound runs as a resolver for the hosted VLAN only (vlan-hosted), listening on 217.169.25.9 and 2001:8b0:bd9:106::1. Access is refused for all addresses except the hosted /29 and /64. All queries are forwarded to the internal recursive resolvers (10.101.1.2, 10.101.1.3).

Hosts on other VLANs use the internal resolvers directly (ns-01, ns-02) and do not go via this Unbound instance.

docs.title: IPv6 Router Advertisements — radvd (services/radvd.nix)
docs.text:
radvd sends Router Advertisements on VLANs 101, 102, 104, 105, and 106. AdvManagedFlag on directs clients to use DHCPv6 (stateful) on VLANs 101, 102, and 104. VLANs 105 and 106 use SLAAC only (AdvManagedFlag off, AdvAutonomous on).

The ULA prefixes for VLANs 101 and 104 are advertised with AdvAutonomous on so clients self-configure stable ULA addresses without needing DHCPv6 for them.

RDNSS records point clients to the internal resolvers on all VLANs.

docs.title: NTP — Chrony (services/ntp.nix)
docs.text:
Chrony synchronises to UK-based stratum-1/2 servers and serves time to:

  • 10.0.0.0/8
  • 2001:8b0:bd9::/48
  • fc00::/7 (ULA)

docs.title: mDNS reflection — Avahi (services/avahi.nix)
docs.text: Avahi reflects mDNS/Bonjour announcements between vlan-private, vlan-lab, and vlan-ha, allowing service discovery (e.g. AirPlay, HomeKit) to work across those three VLANs.

docs.title: UPnP / NAT-PMP — miniupnpd (services/upnp.nix)
docs.text:
miniupnpd (nftables build) manages dynamic port mappings on behalf of clients on vlan-private and vlan-public. The external interface is ppp0.

Port ranges are restricted: only ports 1024–65535 may be mapped, and only for hosts in 10.101.0.0/16 or 10.102.0.0/16. All other mapping requests are denied.

miniupnpd manages its own miniupnpd nftables table at runtime and does not modify the main inet filter or ip nat tables.

docs.title: Suricata IDS (services/suricata.nix)
docs.text: Suricata runs as a passive IDS monitoring vlan-hosted via af-packet mode. ET Open rules are fetched daily via suricata-update and live-reloaded with SIGUSR2. Alerts from eve.json are shipped to Loki via Alloy, filtered to only alert-level events with extracted signature_severity labels.

docs.title: Kernel auditing (services/auditd.nix)
docs.text: auditd is enabled with CIS-inspired rules covering time changes, identity changes, network environment, privilege scope, login/session events, DAC permission changes, mounts, and kernel module load/unload. Events are forwarded to syslog (→ journald → Loki) and kept locally with 5 × 20 MiB rotation.

docs.title: Tailscale VPN (services/tailscale.nix)
docs.text:
Tailscale connects to the self-hosted Headscale server at https://hs.doofnet.uk. The node advertises:

  • All internal routes: 10.0.0.0/8, 2001:8b0:bd9::/48, fddd:d00f:dab0::/48
  • Exit node capability

useRoutingFeatures = "server" enables kernel IP forwarding for Tailscale traffic. Telemetry is disabled (--no-logs-no-support). The firewall is not opened automatically (openFirewall = false); inbound Tailscale traffic is handled by the nftables ruleset.

docs.title: Firewall (services/firewall.nix)
docs.text: NixOS's legacy iptables firewall is disabled; nftables is used exclusively. The ruleset lives in a single table inet filter plus a table ip nat.

docs.title: Named sets
docs.text: Set Contents local4 10.0.0.0/8, 217.169.25.8/29 local6 2001:8b0:bd9::/48, fc00::/7 pub_ns4 Public secondary nameservers — ns-03 (52.19.64.4) and ns-04 (16.60.149.205) ns4/6 Internal resolvers (10.101.1.2/3, their IPv6 counterparts) hosted_out_tcp TCP ports hosted VLAN may use outbound: 22, 23, 25, 53, 70, 79, 80, 123, 443, 1965 hosted_out_udp UDP ports hosted VLAN may use outbound: 53, 123

docs.title: Input chain (policy: drop)
docs.text:
• Loopback always accepted
  • Anti-spoofing: RFC1918 and ULA source addresses dropped on ppp0
  • Established/related accepted; invalid dropped
  • TCP flag validation (drops malformed/scan packets)
  • ICMPv4: echo-request, unreachable, time-exceeded at 10/s
  • ICMPv6: NDP, MLD, transit types at 10/s
  • DHCPv4 (port 67) from all internal VLANs
  • DHCPv6 (port 547) from private, public, lab, hosted VLANs
  • DHCPv6 client replies from ISP on ppp0 (link-local source, port 547→546)
  • DNS (port 53) from public secondary nameservers (pub_ns4: ns-03/04) for zone transfers / NOTIFY
  • DNS (port 53) from hosted VLAN (forwarded to Unbound)
  • NTP (port 123) from local4/local6
  • SSH (port 22) from private VLAN, management interface, and Tailscale only
  • mDNS (port 5353) from private, lab, HA VLANs
  • UPnP SSDP (port 1900) and NAT-PMP/PCP (port 5351) from private and public VLANs
  • Everything else from ppp0 counted and dropped

docs.title: Forward chain (policy: drop)
docs.text:
• Established/related accepted; invalid dropped
  • TCP flag validation
  • ICMPv6 transit types forwarded at 10/s (NDP and MLD are link-local and not forwarded)
  • DNATed connections accepted (ct status dnat accept)
  • DNS forwarded from public, hosted, HA VLANs to internal resolvers
  • mDNS forwarded between private, lab, HA VLANs
  • Private VLAN: unrestricted outbound (full access including to hosted)
  • Lab VLAN: blocked from reaching private and HA VLANs; otherwise unrestricted
  • Tailscale (tailscale0): unrestricted outbound
  • Public VLAN: outbound to WAN; HTTP/HTTPS to hosted; specific access to 10.101.3.20:443
  • Hosted VLAN: restricted outbound to WAN (only hosted_out_tcp/udp); access to svc-01 (10.101.3.20:443) and svc-02 (10.101.3.21:443,9090); Tailscale port 41641
  • WAN → hosted: accepted to 217.169.25.8/29 and 2001:8b0:bd9:106::/64 (publicly routed, no NAT)
  • WAN → private: QBittorrent (TCP/UDP 51413) inbound for IPv6
  • WAN inbound drops counted silently; all other drops logged with nft-forward-drop: prefix

docs.title: Named counters (exported to Prometheus)
docs.text: Counter Meaning fw_wan_input_drop Packets dropped in input chain from ppp0 fw_wan_forward_drop Inbound WAN packets dropped in forward chain fw_hosted_unmatched Hosted VLAN outbound packets that fell through accepts fw_synflood_drop Inbound TCP SYNs dropped by per-source rate limit fw_forward_drop All other forward drops (internal traffic)

docs.title: NAT (table ip nat)
docs.text:
Prerouting DNATs (applied to ppp0 ingress, skipped for the hosted /29):

Destination port Protocol DNAT target Service 443 TCP+UDP 10.101.3.20:8443 HTTPS → svc-01 53 (ns-03/04 src) TCP+UDP 10.101.1.2:53 DNS → ns-01 (zone transfers) 51413 TCP+UDP 10.101.3.16 BitTorrent → QBittorrent 387 (UDP) UDP 10.101.3.21 AARP/AppleTalk → JRouter

Postrouting: all 10.0.0.0/8 traffic leaving ppp0 is masqueraded (the hosted /29 is excluded by the prerouting return).

docs.title: Kernel hardening
docs.text:
The following sysctl settings are applied, mostly following CIS benchmarks:

Setting Value Rationale net.ipv4/6.conf.all.forwarding 1 Required for router operation rp_filter (all/default) 1 Strict reverse-path filtering (anti-spoofing) rp_filter (ppp0) 2 Loose mode for PPPoE asymmetric routing accept_source_route 0 Disable IP source routing (CIS 3.2.1) accept_redirects 0 Reject ICMP redirects (CIS 3.2.2) send_redirects 0 Do not send ICMP redirects (CIS 3.1.2) log_martians 1 Log spoofed/impossible source addresses (CIS 3.2.4) icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts 1 Smurf amplification protection (CIS 3.2.5) tcp_syncookies 1 SYN flood protection (CIS 3.2.8) tcp_rfc1337 1 TIME_WAIT assassination protection (RFC 1337) kernel.dmesg_restrict 1 Restrict dmesg access kernel.kptr_restrict 2 Hide kernel pointers kernel.randomize_va_space 2 Full ASLR (CIS 1.5.3) kernel.yama.ptrace_scope 1 Restrict ptrace to parent processes fs.protected_hardlinks/symlinks 1 Prevent privilege escalation via links fs.suid_dumpable 0 No core dumps for SUID programs (CIS 1.5.1) kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled 1 Disable unprivileged BPF net.core.bpf_jit_harden 2 Harden BPF JIT against info leaks

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
As a doofnet.server host, gw ships metrics to Prometheus via Grafana Alloy and logs to Loki. Additional exporters configured in services/alloy.nix:

  • kea-exporter (port 9547) — Kea DHCP lease and server statistics
  • chrony-exporter (port 9123) — NTP sync status and offset
  • suricata (via eve.json → Loki) — IDS alerts with severity labels

Firewall counters (fw_wan_input_drop, etc.) are collected via the nftables textfile collector and exposed through the node exporter.

docs.title: Service summary
docs.text: Service Package Purpose pppd ppp PPPoE WAN connection to AAISP kea-dhcp4 kea DHCPv4 for VLANs 101, 102, 104, 105 kea-dhcp6 kea DHCPv6 for VLANs 101, 102, 104 kea-dhcp-ddns kea Dynamic DNS updates via TSIG unbound unbound DNS resolver for hosted VLAN only radvd radvd IPv6 router advertisements chrony chrony NTP server avahi avahi mDNS reflection across private/lab/HA miniupnpd miniupnpd UPnP / NAT-PMP port mapping tailscale tailscale Mesh VPN via self-hosted Headscale suricata suricata NIDS monitoring of hosted VLAN auditd auditd Kernel audit logging (CIS-inspired) lldpd lldpd LLDP (trunk interface only) alloy grafana-alloy Metrics and log shipping

docs.title: hs-01: Headscale VPN Controller
docs.text: hs-01 is a NixOS microVM (CID 10) on hyp-01, running on VLAN 106 with a publicly routed IP. It runs Headscale — the self-hosted Tailscale control server — and an embedded DERP relay.

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
Property Value Platform microVM, CID 10, VLAN 106 IPv4 217.169.25.13/29 IPv6 2001:8b0:bd9:106::13/64 Gateway 217.169.25.9 (gw hosted VLAN)

Open ports: 80, 443 (nginx → headscale), 3478 (STUN/DTLS for DERP).

docs.title: Headscale
docs.text:
The Headscale instance at https://hs.doofnet.uk manages the Tailscale mesh network:

Setting Value Server URL https://hs.doofnet.uk:443 Base domain ts.doofnet.uk Magic DNS Enabled DERP region ID 999, "St. Helens" OIDC issuer https://id.doofnet.uk (Pocket-ID) Prefixes 100.64.0.0/10, fd7a:115c:a1e0::/48

docs.title: DNS configuration
docs.text:
Magic DNS is enabled with split DNS:

  • doofnet.uk and incognitus.net resolve to internal DNS servers (10.101.1.2/3)
  • Global fallback uses Cloudflare (1.1.1.1, 1.0.0.1)
  • Search domains: ts.doofnet.uk, int.doofnet.uk, doofnet.uk

docs.title: ACL policy
docs.text: ACLs are defined as a JSON file and loaded by Headscale. Groups: home, vpn, admin. Tag-based routing allows selective access between groups.

docs.title: nginx reverse proxy
docs.text: nginx terminates TLS for hs.doofnet.uk (ACME via Let's Encrypt) and proxies to Headscale on 127.0.0.1:8080. Long timeout (3600s) is set for DERP WebSocket connections.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text: Headscale data is bind-mounted from /persist/headscale to /var/lib/headscale.

docs.title: Service summary
docs.text: Service Package Purpose headscale headscale Tailscale control server nginx nginx TLS termination and reverse proxy alloy grafana-alloy Metrics and log shipping

docs.title: hyp-01: Hypervisor
docs.text: hyp-01 is a KVM-based hypervisor running NixOS. It hosts two categories of virtual machine: NixOS microVMs (declared in the flake, managed by the microvm.nix NixOS module), and QEMU VMs (raw disk images, managed as plain systemd services). Both types share the same bridge-based VLAN network.

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text: HP Prodesk 600 G3 DM, same hardware class as the other base hosts. See docs/base_host.md.

docs.title: Networking
docs.text:
hyp-01 uses a single bridge (br0) with VLAN filtering. The physical uplink (eno1) is a member of the bridge, carrying all VLANs as tagged traffic. Two VLANs are active:

VLAN Name Subnet IPv6 prefix Purpose 101 private 10.101.0.0/16 2001:8b0:bd9:101::/64 Internal services 106 hosted 217.169.25.8/29 2001:8b0:bd9:106::/64 Internet-facing / externally routed

hyp-01's own management address is 10.101.3.22/16 (VLAN 101).

VM tap interfaces are named vm-<vlan>-<identifier> (e.g. vm-101-afp-01). Interfaces matching vm-101-* are added to the bridge with PVID 101 (untagged egress on VLAN 101), and vm-106-* likewise for VLAN 106. VMs therefore receive untagged Ethernet and see their VLAN as a plain L2 segment.

IP forwarding is enabled for both IPv4 and IPv6 so the host can forward packets between its interface and any routed subnets.

docs.title: MicroVMs
docs.text: MicroVMs are NixOS guests declared in the main flake and evaluated as full NixOS configurations. They run under the microvm.nix NixOS module using QEMU as the hypervisor.

docs.title: Host-side configuration (hosts/hyp-01/microvms.nix)
docs.text:
Each guest is registered with microvm.vms.<name> pointing at the flake. The restartIfChanged option controls whether nixos-rebuild on the host restarts a guest when its configuration changes. Most guests set this to false and receive their new configuration on the next reboot; mx-01 sets restartIfChanged = true so mail service is resumed immediately after a config change.

At evaluation time the file cross-references every guest's evaluated NixOS configuration (via inputs.self.nixosConfigurations) to extract its doofnet.microvm.cid value. A NixOS assertion fires if any two guests share a CID, catching conflicts before deployment.

Persistent data directories are created under /srv/data/persist/microvms/<name> by systemd.tmpfiles, and the entire tree is included in borgmatic backups.

docs.title: Guest-side module (modules/doofnet/microvm.nix)
docs.text:
Each guest enables doofnet.microvm and sets:

Option Type Default Description cid int (required) vsock context ID, must be unique across all VMs vlan string (required) VLAN number used in the tap interface name mac string lib.mkMAC hostname MAC address for the tap interface vcpu int 2 Virtual CPU count mem int 1024 Memory in MiB

The module configures the microvm with:

  • Hypervisor: QEMU with KVM acceleration
  • vsock: enabled with SSH access via vsock.ssh.enable; registerWithMachined = true so the guest appears in machinectl
  • Network: single TAP interface (vm-<vlan>-<hostname>) with vhost acceleration
  • Shares (virtiofs):
  • /nix/.ro-store ← /nix/store (read-only, shared from host)
  • /persist ← /srv/data/persist/microvms/<hostname> (writable, per-guest)
  • Persistence: SSH host keys, DH parameters, and ACME certificates are stored under /persist so they survive NixOS rebuilds

docs.title: MAC address derivation (lib/mkmac.nix)
docs.text:
When no explicit MAC is provided, lib.mkMAC hostname generates a deterministic address:

  1. SHA1 hash of the hostname is computed
  2. Bytes 1–3 of the hash become the last three octets
  3. The prefix 02:00:00 is prepended (locally administered, unicast)

This ensures stable MACs without manual assignment.

docs.title: Declared microVMs
docs.text:
Name CID VLAN Role afp-01 11 101 AFP/AppleTalk file server, Netatalk ns-02 13 101 Secondary DNS (BIND) hs-01 10 106 Headscale VPN controller, DERP server web-01 14 106 Nginx web hosting (multiple domains) mx-01 12 106 Mail server (Postfix + Dovecot + DKIM) grf-01 15 101 Grafana monitoring dashboard

All guests use the default resource allocation (2 vCPUs, 1024 MiB RAM).

Guests on VLAN 106 (hs-01, web-01, mx-01) are internet-facing and have public IP addresses in the 217.169.25.8/29 block.

docs.title: QEMU VMs
docs.text: QEMU VMs are raw disk image guests that are not NixOS — they run arbitrary operating systems. They are declared in hosts/hyp-01/qemu_vms.nix and each become a qemu-vm-<name> systemd service.

docs.title: VM definition format
docs.text:
VMs are declared in the vms attrset. Required and optional fields:

Field Required Default Description vlan yes — VLAN number for network placement mac no lib.mkMAC name MAC address tapId no first 8 chars of name Suffix of the tap interface name vcpus no 2 Virtual CPU count memory no 2048 Memory in MiB diskFormat no "vmdk" QEMU disk image format

Disk image path: /srv/data/vms/<name>/disk.<diskFormat>

EFI vars are seeded from the system OVMF package on first boot and stored per-VM at /srv/data/vms/<name>/OVMF_VARS.fd, so EFI settings persist across reboots.

docs.title: Networking
docs.text: Each VM gets a TAP interface named vm-<vlan>-<tapId>. The interface is created in ExecStartPre (running as root via the + prefix) and destroyed in ExecStopPost. Because the interface name follows the vm-<vlan>-* pattern it is automatically picked up by the systemd-networkd match rules and placed onto the correct VLAN.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
The qemu-vm system user and group own all VM processes. The service applies:

  • NoNewPrivileges = true
  • PrivateTmp = true
  • ProtectSystem = strict
  • ReadWritePaths limited to the VM's data directory
  • DeviceAllow for /dev/kvm, /dev/net/tun, /dev/vhost-net

docs.title: Declared QEMU VMs
docs.text: Name VLAN Notes homeassistant 101 Home Assistant OS image (VMDK)

docs.title: Storage layout
docs.text:
/srv/data/
  microvm/          → bind-mounted to /var/lib/microvm (microvm.nix state)
  persist/
    microvms/
      <name>/       → virtiofs "persist" share for each microVM
        ssh_host_ed25519_key
        ssh_host_rsa_key
        dhparams/
        acme/
  vms/
    <name>/         → data directory for each QEMU VM
      disk.<fmt>    → primary disk image
      OVMF_VARS.fd  → per-VM EFI variable store

docs.title: Adding a new microVM
docs.text:
1. Create hosts/<name>/configuration.nix with doofnet.microvm.enable = true, setting a unique cid, the desired vlan, and resource options.
  2. Declare the guest in flake.nix using mkMicrovm: 
    <name> = mkMicrovm "<name>" { };
    The mkMicrovm helper automatically adds inputs.microvm.nixosModules.microvm and modules/doofnet/microvm.nix. Pass extraModules if the guest needs additional modules.
  3. Add <name> = { flake = inputs.self; restartIfChanged = true; } to microvm.vms in hosts/hyp-01/microvms.nix. The CID uniqueness assertion will catch any conflicts at nix flake check time.

docs.title: Adding a new QEMU VM
docs.text:
1. Add an entry to the vms attrset in hosts/hyp-01/qemu_vms.nix with at minimum vlan set.
  2. Place (or symlink) the disk image at /srv/data/vms/<name>/disk.<diskFormat>.
  3. nixos-rebuild switch on hyp-01 will create the data directory, generate the tap interface config, and start the qemu-vm-<name> service.

docs.title: macOS Setup
docs.text: Steps to set up a macOS workstation for working with this repository, including Nix installation and LSP tooling for editor support.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Install Nix using the Determinate Nix Installer:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf -L https://install.determinate.systems/nix | sh -s -- install

This provides a deterministic Nix installation with automatic updates and a graceful uninstall path.

  1. Restart your terminal, then install development tools:
nix profile install nixpkgs#nixfmt-rfc-style
nix profile install nixpkgs#statix
nix profile install nixpkgs#deadnix
nix profile install nixpkgs#nixd
nix profile install nixpkgs#nil
  1. (Optional) Install agenix for secret management: 
    nix profile install nixpkgs#agenix

docs.title: VS Code / VSCodium
docs.text: Install the jnoortheen.nix-ide extension for Nix language support. The extension auto-detects nil and nixd from PATH.

docs.title: Neovim
docs.text:
Use nickel-nix for tree-sitter parsing and nil or nixd as the LSP server. The following plugins are recommended:

  • nickel-nix (tree-sitter grammar)
  • neovim/nvim-lspconfig (LSP client configuration)

docs.title: Zed
docs.text:
Nix language support is built-in. The LSP server must be on PATH:

{
  "lsp": {
    "nil": {
      "binary": {
        "path": "nil"
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: First use
docs.text:
Clone the repository and verify the flake:

git clone https://github.com/nikdoof/nixos-homeprod.git
cd nixos-homeprod
nix flake check

docs.title: mx-01: Mail Server
docs.text: mx-01 is a NixOS microVM (CID 12) on hyp-01, running on VLAN 106 with a publicly routed IP address. It handles inbound and outbound email for two domains using Postfix (MTA) and Dovecot (IMAP/LMTP), with spam/virus scanning, DKIM signing, DMARC policy enforcement, and SPF validation all handled by Rspamd.

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
Property Value Platform microVM, CID 12, VLAN 106 IPv4 217.169.25.11/29 IPv6 2001:8b0:bd9:106::3/64 Gateway 217.169.25.9 (gw hosted VLAN) DNS 217.169.25.9, 2001:8b0:bd9:106::1 (Unbound on gw)

Open TCP ports: 25 (SMTP/postscreen), 465 (SMTPS submission), 587 (submission), 993 (IMAPS).

docs.title: Hosted domains
docs.text:
Virtual mailboxes are served for two domains:

  • doofnet.uk
  • dimension.sh

The host's own FQDN (mx-01.doofnet.uk) is the only entry in mydestination. All mail for the virtual domains is delivered via LMTP to Dovecot.

docs.title: TLS / ACME
docs.text: A single TLS certificate covers mx-01.doofnet.uk, obtained via ACME DNS-01 challenge using the DigitalOcean API. After renewal, ACLs are applied so that dovecot2 and postfix can read the certificate files without running as acme, and all three services (postfix, dovecot2, rspamd) are reloaded. The certificate is used by Postfix (inbound and outbound), Dovecot (IMAP), and Rspamd (DKIM/DMARC verification).

docs.title: Listeners
docs.text:
Port Service Auth required Notes 25 postscreen No Pre-screens before reaching smtpd 587 submission Yes (SASL) TLS enforced (encrypt) 465 submissions Yes (SASL) TLS wrapper mode

Port 25 is handled by postscreen (not smtpd directly). The smtp_inet master.cf entry is overridden to run postscreen, which passes clean connections to a smtpd pass service. The dnsblog and tlsproxy helper processes are declared to support postscreen DNSBL lookups.

docs.title: Relay policy
docs.text:
permit_mynetworks → permit_sasl_authenticated → reject_unauth_destination

mynetworks covers loopback, the private VLAN (10.101.0.0/16), and the hosted /29 and /64, meaning infrastructure hosts can relay without authentication.

docs.title: Inbound TLS
docs.text:
• Security level: may (opportunistic; enforced for submission ports)
  • Minimum protocol: TLSv1.2
  • Session cache: btree:/var/lib/postfix/data/smtpd_scache
  • smtpd_tls_received_header = yes — adds Received: trace with TLS info

docs.title: Outbound TLS (DANE)
docs.text: Outbound delivery uses smtp_tls_security_level = dane with smtp_dns_support_level = dnssec. For receiving servers that publish TLSA records, Postfix performs DANE-verified TLS; for those that don't, it falls back to opportunistic TLS. The DNSSEC-validating resolvers on mx-01 (Unbound on gw) are essential for this to work.

docs.title: Postscreen
docs.text:
Postscreen pre-screens inbound SMTP connections before they consume smtpd resources:

Check Action Detail DNSBL threshold ≥ 2 enforce zen.spamhaus.org*2, dnsbl.dronebl.org*2, bl.spamcop.net*1 Blacklist action drop Silent drop for blacklisted IPs permit_mynetworks pass Skips all postscreen checks for trusted networks

Notes: Bare newline checking is intentionally disabled at the postscreen level (postscreen_bare_newline_enable = no). Keeping it enabled caused postscreen to defer the BARLF check past STARTTLS negotiation, preventing TLS proxy from forwarding connections to smtpd (resulting in 450 4.3.2 errors for legitimate senders like O365). SMTP request smuggling protection is enforced by smtpd_forbid_bare_newline = yes at the smtpd level instead, which is not subject to the same TLS proxy interaction.

Passed connections are cached in btree:/var/lib/postfix/data/postscreen_cache.

docs.title: HELO, sender, and recipient restrictions
docs.text:
HELO restrictions (in order):

  1. permit_mynetworks
  2. reject_non_fqdn_helo_hostname
  3. reject_invalid_helo_hostname
  4. reject_rhsbl_helo dbl.spamhaus.org
  5. permit

Sender restrictions (in order):

  1. permit_mynetworks, permit_sasl_authenticated
  2. reject_non_fqdn_sender, reject_unknown_sender_domain
  3. reject_rhsbl_sender dbl.spamhaus.org
  4. permit

On port 25 (behind postscreen), the sender restrictions are overridden to be simpler since SASL is not available and reject_rhsbl_sender can't be applied via the master.cf -o syntax (no spaces allowed). Rspamd provides equivalent checks via its milter:

  1. permit_mynetworks
  2. reject_non_fqdn_sender, reject_unknown_sender_domain
  3. permit

Recipient restrictions (in order):

  1. reject_invalid_hostname, reject_unknown_recipient_domain
  2. reject_unauth_pipelining, reject_multi_recipient_bounce
  3. permit_mynetworks
  4. reject_non_fqdn_recipient, reject_unauth_destination
  5. RBL checks: dnsbl.dronebl.org, zen.spamhaus.org, bl.spamcop.net, b.barracudacentral.org, dnsbl-1.uceprotect.net
  6. permit

SPF and additional RBL checks are handled by Rspamd rather than Postfix policy services.

docs.title: CVE-2023-51764 (SMTP request smuggling)
docs.text: smtpd_forbid_bare_newline = yes is set globally. mynetworks hosts are exempted via smtpd_forbid_bare_newline_exclusions.

docs.title: Rate limiting
docs.text:
Per-client limits measured over a 60-second window:

Limit Value Connection rate 10/min Message rate 100/min Recipient rate 100/min Auth attempt rate 10/min

docs.title: Bounce / error handling
docs.text: Setting Value Rationale bounce_queue_lifetime 1d Short bounce window reduces backscatter risk smtpd_soft_error_limit 3 Delay after 3 soft errors smtpd_hard_error_limit 10 Drop after 10 hard errors smtpd_error_sleep_time 1s Slow-down delay per error

docs.title: Milters
docs.text:
A single Rspamd proxy milter is applied to both smtpd_milters and non_smtpd_milters:

inet:127.0.0.1:11332

Rspamd handles all mail filtering functions previously split across three separate milters:

Function Previously Now DKIM signing OpenDKIM Rspamd dkim_signing module DKIM verify OpenDKIM Rspamd built-in DMARC OpenDMARC Rspamd dmarc module SPF policyd-spf Rspamd built-in Spam scoring — Rspamd (bayes, neural, rules) Greylisting — Rspamd greylist module Rate limit — Rspamd ratelimit module

milter_macro_daemon_name = ORIGINATING is set on submission ports so Rspamd can distinguish outbound authenticated mail from inbound.

milter_default_action = accept means a milter failure does not block delivery.

docs.title: Aliases
docs.text:
Local system aliases:

Alias Destination root root-mail@m.tensixtyone.com inbox paperless, household@williams.id nikdoof andy@williams.id salkunh jo@williams.id

Virtual aliases forward root@ for internal subdomains to root-mail@m.tensixtyone.com.

docs.title: Rspamd
docs.text: Rspamd replaces OpenDKIM, OpenDMARC, and policyd-spf, consolidating all mail filtering into a single milter-backed service. It uses Valkey as its backing store and exposes a controller UI on 127.0.0.1:11334.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Two worker processes run:

Worker Socket Purpose Proxy 127.0.0.1:11332 Milter interface for Postfix; self-scans Controller 127.0.0.1:11334 Web UI (admin restricted to localhost)

The proxy worker is configured as a self-scan upstream — it receives messages from Postfix, scans them locally, and returns the verdict. No separate scanning nodes.

docs.title: DKIM signing
docs.text:
Rspamd replaces OpenDKIM for DKIM signing. Configuration is in dkim_signing.conf:

Setting Value Selector 658553b1d7df5d1bc6bdb2176d87b585a0ad54ce (SHA1 of mx-01.doofnet.uk) Key path /persist/opendkim/keys/<selector>.private Domains doofnet.uk, dimension.sh (all virtual domains) Condition Sign all outbound mail (return true)

The key files are the same ones originally created for OpenDKIM, stored on /persist for survival across rebuilds. The DKIM public key must be published as a DNS TXT record at <selector>._domainkey.<domain> for each virtual domain.

docs.title: DMARC
docs.text:
Rspamd's dmarc module is configured with reporting enabled and rejection actions:

Setting Value Reporting enabled true Reject action for DMARC fail reject:reject

The DMARC policy is monitoring mode (p=none) at the DNS level. Rspamd logs DMARC failures but applies reject only within its own scoring system — the actual DNS p= policy controls what receiving servers do.

docs.title: SPF
docs.text: SPF checking is built into Rspamd's core scanning — no separate policy daemon needed. Rspamd evaluates SPF for each inbound message and factors the result into the spam score.

docs.title: Greylisting
docs.text: Greylisting is enabled via the greylist module (config in greylist.conf). Note the config file is named greylist.conf, not greylisting.conf — the upstream module expects the shorter name.

docs.title: Neural network
docs.text:
Rspamd's neural network classifier is configured with Valkey as its backend:

servers = "127.0.0.1:6379";

This enables per-user neural classification models persisted in Valkey.

docs.title: MX check
docs.text: mx_check is enabled: Rspamd verifies the sender domain has reachable MX records. Mail from domains without valid MX configuration is scored accordingly.

docs.title: Per-user outbound rate limiting
docs.text:
Rspamd enforces per-user rate limits on authenticated outbound mail via ratelimit.conf:

Setting Value Burst 200 Rate 100/hr

This prevents compromised accounts from flooding outbound queues.

docs.title: Settings for authenticated users
docs.text:
Rspamd's settings.conf defines a high-priority rule for authenticated users that:

  • Disables RBL, SPF, and neural checks (trusted outbound)
  • Raises reject threshold to 100 (practically disables rejection)
  • Raises greylist threshold to 50

This ensures authenticated outbound mail is not delayed or blocked by false positives.

docs.title: Redis/Valkey
docs.text:
Rspamd uses Valkey (a Redis-compatible key-value store) for:

  • Greylisting state
  • Neural network classification models
  • Rate limiting counters
  • Statistics and learning data

Configuration is in redis.conf:

servers = "127.0.0.1:6379";

Valkey runs as a redis.servers.rspamd systemd unit with AOF + RDB persistence, writing to /persist/valkey via a bind mount at /var/lib/redis-rspamd.

docs.title: Milter headers
docs.text:
Rspamd adds the following headers to processed messages (config in milter_headers.conf):

  • X-Spam-Score — numeric spam score
  • X-Spam-Level — visual star representation of score
  • Authentication-Results — SPF/DKIM/DMARC auth status

docs.title: Dovecot
docs.text: Dovecot provides IMAP access and LMTP delivery for Postfix. POP3 is disabled. PAM authentication is disabled.

docs.title: Virtual users
docs.text:
All mailboxes are virtual users authenticated against an passwd-file stored in an age-encrypted secret (secrets/mx01DovecotPasswd.age). Usernames are normalised to lowercase full email addresses (auth_username_format = %Lu).

Mail is stored under:

/persist/vmail/<domain>/<user>/Maildir/

The vmail system user owns all mailbox files. On service start, an ExecStartPre step chowns the entire mail store to vmail:vmail.

docs.title: Mailboxes
docs.text:
The following IMAP special-use folders are auto-created for all users:

Folder RFC role Auto-expunge All \All Never Archive \Archive Never Drafts \Drafts Never Flagged \Flagged Never Junk \Junk 60 days Sent \Sent Never Trash \Trash 60 days

docs.title: LMTP socket
docs.text: Dovecot exposes an LMTP listener at /var/spool/postfix/dovecot-lmtp (inside the Postfix chroot), owned postfix:postfix. Postfix delivers via lmtp:unix:/var/spool/postfix/dovecot-lmtp.

docs.title: Authentication socket
docs.text: The SASL auth socket is at /var/spool/postfix/auth, owned postfix:postfix. Postfix uses this for both submission port SASL and inbound smtpd SASL.

docs.title: TLS
docs.text: Minimum TLS version: TLSv1.2. Server cipher preference is enabled.

docs.title: Plugins / features
docs.text:
Full-text search (FTS) via fts_flatcurve provides indexed IMAP SEARCH across all mailboxes. Two tokenizers are configured:

Tokenizer Algorithm Max length generic simple 30 chars email-address (default) 100 chars

Token filters for indexing and search are language-specific:

Language Filters English lowercase, snowball (stemming), english-possessive, stopwords German normalizer-icu, snowball, stopwords

Supported languages: English (en), German (de).

Listescape is enabled in IMAP to allow mailbox names containing characters that would normally be interpreted as hierarchy separators (e.g., foo.bar or foo/bar). The plugin allows escaping these characters so they can be used literally in mailbox names.

IMAP ACL (Access Control List) enables shared mailbox access. Users can subscribe to shared mailboxes under the Shared/ namespace using the / hierarchy separator.

Shared mailboxes are configured via doofnet.mailserver.sharedAccess. Currently nikdoof@doofnet.uk has access to:

  • paperless@doofnet.uk
  • dmarc-reports@doofnet.uk
  • hello@nikdoof.com

The shared namespace uses a dedicated INDEX directory to avoid locking conflicts.

docs.title: Sieve spam filtering
docs.text:
A global sieve_before script (spam-to-junk.sieve) is applied to all LMTP deliveries:

if header :contains "X-Spam-Level" "*****" {
  fileinto :create "Junk";
  stop;
}

Mail with an X-Spam-Level of 5+ stars (spam score ≥ ~5) is automatically filed into the Junk folder. The Sieve plugin is loaded per-protocol for LMTP only (the pigeonhole plugin is compiled for lda/lmtp and cannot be loaded in IMAP contexts).

Per-user Sieve scripts are supported but not currently configured — users can place custom scripts at ~/.dovecot.sieve or in the ~/sieve/ directory.

docs.title: Metrics endpoint
docs.text:
Dovecot exposes an HTTP stats endpoint on port 9166 (service stats). The following metrics are collected:

Metric Filter Grouped by auth_success auth_request_finished, success=yes — imap_command imap_command_finished cmd_name, tagged_reply_state smtp_command smtp_server_command_finished cmd_name, status_code, duration histogram mail_delivery mail_delivery_finished duration histogram

docs.title: Fail2ban
docs.text: The dovecot jail is enabled via doofnet.fail2ban.jails.dovecot = true, banning IPs that exceed failed IMAP authentication thresholds.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text:
mx-01 uses the standard microVM /persist virtiofs share for state that must survive rebuilds:

Path Contents /persist/vmail/ All user mailboxes (Maildir) /persist/opendkim/keys/ DKIM private keys /persist/valkey/ Valkey AOF + RDB dumps /persist/ssh_host_* SSH host keys (standard microVM) /persist/acme/ ACME certificates

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
Three exporters run locally and are scraped by Alloy:

Exporter Port Job Metrics prometheus-postfix-exporter 9154 postfix Queue lengths, delivery rates, error counts (systemd journal) Dovecot stats HTTP 9166 dovecot Auth, IMAP command, SMTP command, delivery metrics prometheus-dmarc-exporter auto dmarc DMARC report pass/fail counts (reads IMAP mailbox dmarc-reports@doofnet.uk)

The DMARC exporter connects to Dovecot via IMAP using credentials loaded as a systemd credential file (compatible with DynamicUser), reading aggregate DMARC reports delivered to the dmarc-reports@doofnet.uk mailbox.

docs.title: DNS records required
docs.text:
The following DNS records must be published for correct mail operation:

Zone Name Type Value / Notes doofnet.uk @ MX mx-01.doofnet.uk dimension.sh @ MX mx-01.doofnet.uk doofnet.uk @ TXT SPF record (v=spf1 mx ~all or similar) dimension.sh @ TXT SPF record doofnet.uk <selector>._domainkey TXT DKIM public key (selector = SHA1 of mx-01.doofnet.uk) dimension.sh <selector>._domainkey TXT DKIM public key doofnet.uk _dmarc TXT v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@doofnet.uk (monitoring mode) dimension.sh _dmarc TXT DMARC policy Reverse 11 in 8-15.25.169.217.in-addr.arpa PTR mx-01.doofnet.uk.

The DKIM selector is derived at build time as the SHA1 hash of the string mx-01.doofnet.uk and is therefore stable as long as the hostname does not change.

docs.title: Raspberry Pi
docs.text: Steps to build and flash a NixOS SD card image for aarch64-linux hosts such as ns-01.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• A build system capable of emulating aarch64-linux (a NixOS host is recommended)
  • Nix
  • A nixosConfiguration that imports sd-image-aarch64.nix from Nixpkgs (see flake.nix and hosts/ns-01/)

docs.title: Build system configuration
docs.text:
Add the following to the build system's NixOS configuration to enable aarch64 emulation:

boot.binfmt.emulatedSystems = [ "aarch64-linux" ];
nix.settings.extra-platforms = [
  "aarch64-linux"
  "arm-linux"
];

docs.title: Building an SD card image
docs.text:
Run nix build targeting the sdImage output of the relevant flake configuration:

nikdoof@svc-02:~/ > nix build --refresh github:nikdoof/nixos-homeprod#nixosConfigurations.ns-01.config.system.build.sdImage
nikdoof@svc-02:~/ > ls result/sd-image
nixos-image-sd-card-25.11.20260207.23d72da-aarch64-linux.img.zst

This produces a standard .img.zst compressed image that can be written to an SD card with a tool such as dd or Raspberry Pi Imager.

docs.title: svc-02: Monitoring Host
docs.text: svc-02 is the monitoring and network services host. It runs the observability stack (Prometheus, Loki, Grafana), network boot services, and JRouter (AppleTalk routing).

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text: HP ProDesk 600 G3 DM — see docs/base_host.md.

docs.text: Service Port(s) Purpose Prometheus 9090 Metrics aggregation from all hosts Loki 3100 Log aggregation from all hosts Promtail — Agent for log shipping (legacy, replaced by Alloy on most hosts) Grafana 3000 Dashboarding (local, grf-01 is the primary UI) Blackbox exporter 9115 External endpoint monitoring Graphite exporter 9109 Graphite metrics compatibility HCloud exporter 9234 Hetzner Cloud API metrics Unpoller — UniFi AP metrics

docs.text: Service Purpose JRouter AppleTalk/DDP routing for afp-01 TFTP PXE boot files (iPXE, undionly) for DHCP on gw Unifi Controller UniFi network management AAISP exporter AAISP usage metrics (via aaisp-chaos flake)

docs.title: Prometheus federation
docs.text: svc-02 is the central Prometheus server for the entire homelab. All hosts ship metrics via Grafana Alloy's remote_write to https://metrics.doofnet.uk/prometheus/api/v1/write, which is reverse-proxied to the local Prometheus instance.

docs.title: JRouter (AppleTalk routing)
docs.text: JRouter is a custom Go service (built from packages/jrouter.nix) that enables AppleTalk/DDP routing between VLANs, supporting the AppleTalk Phase 2 network on afp-01.

docs.title: TFTP (PXE boot)
docs.text: The TFTP server serves PXE boot images (ipxe.efi for UEFI, undionly.kpxe for BIOS) for network booting on VLANs 101 and 104. svc-02 acts as the next-server for VLAN 101's DHCP PXE configuration.

docs.title: Networking
docs.text: Property Value IPv4 10.101.3.21/16 IPv6 2001:8b0:bd9:101::21/64 ULA fddd:d00f:dab0:101::21/64 DNS suffix svc.doofnet.uk

docs.title: Cross-compilation
docs.text: svc-02 has doofnet.cross_compile = true, enabling aarch64-linux emulation via binfmt. This allows building aarch64 NixOS images (e.g. ns-01 SD card images) directly on this host. It also holds the Nix signing key (secrets/svc02NixSigningKey.age) for signing substitute cache entries.

docs.text: Site content is deployed via rsync over SSH by the deploy system user. The user's ForceCommand is restricted to rrsync /persist/sites, preventing access outside the sites directory.
